description: Historical fiction requires characters whose identity is constituted by their period's social position, settings with operative material conditions, and conflicts caused by historical conditions rather than merely set against them.

character_constraints.description: A character whose identity is constituted by the period's social position — class, gender, profession, and era shape every choice.
A real historical person rendered with documented accuracy — not a cameo but a participant.
A character who personifies the era's power structure — lord, enslaver, priest, officer.

relationship_constraints.description: Hierarchy of the era shapes every relationship — master/servant, lord/vassal, colonizer/colonized.
A relationship that crosses social boundaries of the era, creating tension specific to the period.

place_constraints.description: A setting where material conditions of the era are operative — technology, transportation, communication, sanitation.
Court, estate, battlefield, parliament, or temple — the physical seat of period authority.

object_constraints.description: A letter, writ, edict, or order carrying time-bound authority and consequences.
An object that embodies social power — seal, crown, deed, weapon of the era.
Weaponry consistent with the era — its capabilities and limitations shape conflict.

element_rules.description: The central conflict must be caused by historical conditions, not merely set against them — remove the period and the story should collapse.
element_rules.testable_condition: The inciting incident and climax are both directly caused by conditions specific to the historical period.

element_rules.description: Characters must think within the conceptual framework of their era — no anachronistic moral vocabulary or modern sensibility.
element_rules.testable_condition: Character dialogue and decisions reflect the values and knowledge available in the depicted period.
